news_summary: Advancements in renewable energy technology, increased scientific engagement in climate action, breakthrough discoveries in methane reduction, and improved understanding of endangered species habitats highlight today's positive environmental progress.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans le domaine des technologies liées aux énergies renouvelables, l'engagement scientifique accru en faveur de la lutte contre le changement climatique, les découvertes majeures en matière de réduction des émissions de méthane et une meilleure compréhension des habitats des espèces menacées de disparition témoignent des avancées environnementales positives observées aujourd'hui.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la tecnología de las energías renovables, una mayor implicación científica en la lucha contra el cambio climático, los descubrimientos revolucionarios en materia de reducción de metano y una mejor comprensión de los hábitats de las especies en peligro de extinción ponen de relieve los avances medioambientales positivos que se están logrando en la actualidad.

articles.title: Continuously graded-doped SnO<sub>2</sub> for efficient n–i–p perovskite solar cells
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 30 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10587-4Continuously graded-doped SnO2 for efficient n–i–p perovskite solar cells
articles.content: Conventional n–i–p architecture remains a robust platform for scalable perovskite photovoltaics1,2, yet its steady-state efficiency has stagnated at ~26% (ref.3), lagging behind p–i–n counterparts4. This performance gap arises from persistent non-radiative recombination at textured electron transport layer (ETL)/perovskite interfaces, yet the underlying physical origin remains unclear. Here, we uncover that these losses originate from the synergistic combination of band misalignment and electron accumulation at the buried interface. To address this dual challenge, we develop a continuously graded n+/n-doped SnO 2 ETL through a ligand-competitive binding strategy, which enables spatially defined doping that creates a built-in electric field. This graded architecture simultaneously minimizes band offset and accelerates electron extraction, thereby effectively suppressing the cross-interface recombination. The resulting n–i–p perovskite solar cells (PSCs) achieve a certified steady-state power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 27.17% (27.50% in reverse scan), the highest for n–i–p PSCs reported to date. The scalability of this strategy is further demonstrated by achieving a PCE of 25.79% for a 1 cm2 device and 23.33% for a perovskite module with a 16.02 cm2 aperture area. This work establishes a generalized paradigm for energy-band engineering in metal-oxide transport layers, overcoming a fundamental efficiency bottleneck in conventional perovskite photovoltaics.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in perovskite solar cell technology, achieving record-high efficiency in n–i–p architecture, which can lead to more scalable and efficient renewable energy solutions. The research addresses a fundamental bottleneck in solar cell performance, with demonstrated scalability and potential broad impact on clean energy adoption.
articles.personality_title: New design boosts efficiency of perovskite solar cells to record levels
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Perovskite solar cells are a promising type of solar technology that can convert sunlight into electricity. One common design, called n–i–p, has been popular but its efficiency has stopped improving around 26%, which is lower than other designs.

**What happened** – Scientists discovered that two problems at the boundary between layers inside the solar cell cause energy loss. To fix this, they created a new layer made of tin oxide (SnO2) with a special way of adding tiny amounts of chemicals (called doping) that change gradually. This new layer helps move electrons better and reduces energy loss.

**Impact** – Using this new design, the solar cells reached an efficiency of 27.17%, the highest ever reported for this type of cell. They also showed the design works on larger solar panels, not just tiny lab samples. This means it could help make better and cheaper solar panels in the future.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely work on making bigger solar panels using this new layer and test how long they last outside the lab. This step is important to bring the new design to real-world solar energy systems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new tin oxide layer design improves perovskite solar cells, reaching record efficiency and showing promise for larger, more practical solar panels.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau design améliore l'efficacité des cellules solaires pérovskites à un niveau record
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cellules solaires pérovskites sont une technologie prometteuse qui transforme la lumière du soleil en électricité. Une conception courante, appelée n–i–p, est populaire mais son efficacité s'est arrêtée autour de 26%, ce qui est moins que d'autres modèles.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert que deux problèmes à la frontière entre les couches internes de la cellule causent des pertes d'énergie. Pour résoudre cela, ils ont créé une nouvelle couche en oxyde d'étain (SnO2) avec une méthode spéciale de dopage progressif. Cette couche aide à mieux déplacer les électrons et réduit les pertes d'énergie.

**Impact** – Avec ce nouveau design, les cellules solaires ont atteint une efficacité de 27,17%, la plus haute jamais rapportée pour ce type de cellule. Ils ont aussi montré que la conception fonctionne sur des panneaux solaires plus grands, pas seulement sur de petits échantillons. Cela pourrait aider à fabriquer des panneaux solaires meilleurs et moins chers.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement fabriquer des panneaux solaires plus grands avec cette nouvelle couche et tester leur durée de vie en conditions réelles. C’est important pour utiliser cette nouvelle conception dans les systèmes solaires du quotidien.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle couche en oxyde d’étain améliore les cellules solaires pérovskites, atteignant une efficacité record et montrant un potentiel pour des panneaux solaires plus grands et pratiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo diseño aumenta la eficiencia de las células solares de perovskita a niveles récord
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las células solares de perovskita son una tecnología prometedora que convierte la luz solar en electricidad. Un diseño común, llamado n–i–p, ha sido popular pero su eficiencia se ha estancado alrededor del 26%, menor que otros diseños.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos descubrieron que dos problemas en la frontera entre capas dentro de la célula causan pérdida de energía. Para solucionarlo, crearon una nueva capa de óxido de estaño (SnO2) con una forma especial de dopaje gradual. Esta capa ayuda a mover mejor los electrones y reduce la pérdida de energía.

**Impacto** – Con este nuevo diseño, las células solares alcanzaron una eficiencia del 27,17%, la más alta reportada para este tipo de células. También demostraron que funciona en paneles solares más grandes, no solo en muestras pequeñas. Esto podría ayudar a fabricar paneles solares mejores y más baratos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente trabajarán en hacer paneles solares más grandes con esta nueva capa y probarán su durabilidad fuera del laboratorio. Esto es clave para llevar el diseño a sistemas solares reales.

**Frase clave** – Una nueva capa de óxido de estaño mejora las células solares de perovskita, logrando eficiencia récord y mostrando potencial para paneles solares más grandes y prácticos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a layered crystal-like structure symbolizing a perovskite solar cell, with a smoothly graded gradient flowing through translucent, textured mineral layers representing the continuously doped SnO₂ electron transport layer, where gentle light beams pass through and accelerate upward, illustrating enhanced electron extraction and minimized energy loss at the interfaces.

articles.title: ‘Beyond COP’ climate summit puts scientists at the centre of the action
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 30 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01399-7Researchers presented ways for nations to phase out fossil fuels at an international meeting of countries ready to take action.
articles.content:
Colombian President Gustavo Petro spoke on 28 April at a climate summit hosted by his country.Credit: Ivan Valencia/AP Photo/Alamy

Climate scientists, who have warned of the dangers of global warming for decades, have found some countries to listen. This week, representatives of more than 50 nations gathered in Santa Marta, Colombia, at what was billed as the first global summit on phasing out fossil fuels. One of the first orders of business was to launch a panel of scientists that will advise those countries on how to shift to clean energy.

How to fight climate change without the US: a guide to global action

“Here, you have a coalition of governments that decided they actually want to be informed by the science,” says Margaretha Wewerinke-Singh, an international climate-change law specialist at the University of Amsterdam.

The landmark meeting, which began on 24 April and concluded yesterday, was proposed during last year’s United Nations COP30 climate summit in Belém, Brazil. Oil-producing nations such as Saudi Arabia reportedly opposed attempts at that gathering to create a road map to cut the use of fossil fuels, which are the main source of global greenhouse-gas emissions and the largest contributor to climate change.

Frustrated, the governments of Colombia and the Netherlands announced that they would host the First Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels this year, independent of the UN’s COP climate summits. Countries that have expressed an openness to creating a road map — including Australia, Cambodia and Mexico — were invited. Oil-producing nations that have opposed such efforts were not.

In Santa Marta, not only was the new panel — called the Science Panel for the Global Energy Transition (SPGET) — launched, but a separate group of researchers also took centre stage on 24 April to release a report listing 12 high-level actions that nations can take to support a fossil-fuel phaseout.

Researchers say it’s refreshing to have an international forum where they are free to make ambitious recommendations. What happens at the UN climate summits is that, “because the governments are the final decision makers on what goes out to the public, there is a lot of filtering” of science advice, says Gilberto Jannuzzi, an energy-transition specialist at the State University of Campinas in São Paulo, Brazil. “At the end, I think we found a smaller audience, but an audience that considers that we have something relevant to them.”

Practical solutions

The report, crafted by 24 researchers in consultation with hundreds of other scientists from several countries over the three months leading up to the meeting, does not attempt to systematically synthesize all of the scientific knowledge on transitioning to clean energy, says Frank Jotzo, a climate-change economist at the Australian National University in Canberra, who was part of the editorial team. That’s the role of the UN Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), which produces reports that are meant to be neutral and inform policy without directly making recommendations. “We’re not trying to replicate the IPCC here. We’re trying to give some practical policy insights” based on science, Jotzo says.

Global greenhouse-gas emissions are still rising: when will they peak?

For example, the report recommends that countries ban new fossil fuel infrastructure and phase out fossil-fuel subsidies, such as tax breaks or funding that lower the cost of producing oil, gas and coal. At the same time, it calls for financial incentives to put clean-energy sources in place.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant international climate summit focused on phasing out fossil fuels, with over 50 nations participating and the launch of a scientific advisory panel to guide clean energy transition. This represents a meaningful and practical step toward addressing climate change with broad global implications and tangible policy recommendations.
articles.personality_title: New global summit puts science at the heart of ending fossil fuels
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many countries have struggled to agree on how to reduce the use of fossil fuels, which cause climate change. Some big oil-producing countries have opposed strong plans to cut fossil fuel use at United Nations meetings.

**What happened** – From April 24 to 28, more than 50 nations met in Santa Marta, Colombia, for the first global summit focused on phasing out fossil fuels. Colombia and the Netherlands hosted the meeting after the United Nations COP30 summit faced opposition. At this summit, a new group called the Science Panel for the Global Energy Transition (SPGET) was created to advise countries on how to switch to clean energy. Researchers also shared a report with 12 clear actions countries can take to stop using fossil fuels.

**Impact** – This summit is important because it brings scientists directly into the decision-making process, without governments filtering their advice. It offers practical suggestions, like banning new fossil fuel projects and ending subsidies that make fossil fuels cheaper. At the same time, it encourages financial support for clean energy. This approach helps countries work together with clear, science-based steps to fight climate change.

**What's next step** – The new scientific panel will continue advising governments on energy choices. Countries involved will consider the recommended actions as they plan how to reduce fossil fuel use. This summit may inspire more countries to join future meetings and speed up the global shift to clean energy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Over 50 countries launched a new science-driven effort in Colombia to guide the world’s move away from fossil fuels toward cleaner energy sources.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau sommet mondial place la science au cœur de la fin des combustibles fossiles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux pays ont eu du mal à s'entendre sur la manière de réduire l'utilisation des combustibles fossiles, responsables du changement climatique. Certains grands pays producteurs de pétrole ont rejeté des plans forts lors des réunions des Nations Unies.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Du 24 au 28 avril, plus de 50 nations se sont réunies à Santa Marta, en Colombie, pour le premier sommet mondial consacré à l'abandon des combustibles fossiles. La Colombie et les Pays-Bas ont organisé cette rencontre après l'opposition rencontrée au sommet COP30 de l'ONU. Un nouveau groupe appelé le Panel scientifique pour la transition énergétique mondiale (SPGET) a été créé pour conseiller les pays sur la transition vers l'énergie propre. Des chercheurs ont aussi présenté un rapport proposant 12 actions claires pour cesser l'utilisation des combustibles fossiles.

**Impact** – Ce sommet est important car il intègre directement les scientifiques dans la prise de décision, sans que les gouvernements ne filtrent leurs conseils. Il propose des solutions pratiques, comme interdire les nouveaux projets liés aux combustibles fossiles et supprimer les subventions qui les rendent moins chers. En même temps, il encourage le soutien financier pour les énergies propres. Cette méthode aide les pays à agir ensemble avec des étapes basées sur la science pour lutter contre le changement climatique.

**Prochaine étape** – Le nouveau panel scientifique continuera de conseiller les gouvernements sur leurs choix énergétiques. Les pays participants examineront les actions recommandées pour réduire l'usage des combustibles fossiles. Ce sommet pourrait inciter plus de pays à rejoindre les prochaines réunions et accélérer la transition mondiale vers les énergies propres.

**En résumé** – Plus de 50 pays ont lancé en Colombie un effort scientifique pour guider la transition mondiale des combustibles fossiles vers des sources d'énergie plus propres.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva cumbre global pone a la ciencia en el centro para eliminar los combustibles fósiles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos países han tenido dificultades para ponerse de acuerdo sobre cómo reducir el uso de combustibles fósiles, que causan el cambio climático. Algunos grandes países productores de petróleo se opusieron a planes fuertes en las reuniones de las Naciones Unidas.

**Qué pasó** – Del 24 al 28 de abril, más de 50 naciones se reunieron en Santa Marta, Colombia, para la primera cumbre global enfocada en eliminar los combustibles fósiles. Colombia y los Países Bajos organizaron esta reunión tras la oposición en la Cumbre COP30 de la ONU. Se creó un nuevo grupo llamado Panel Científico para la Transición Global de Energía (SPGET) para asesorar a los países sobre cómo cambiar a energía limpia. Investigadores también presentaron un informe con 12 acciones claras para dejar de usar combustibles fósiles.

**Impacto** – Esta cumbre es importante porque pone a los científicos directamente en el proceso de toma de decisiones, sin que los gobiernos filtren sus consejos. Ofrece sugerencias prácticas, como prohibir nuevos proyectos de combustibles fósiles y eliminar subsidios que abaratan estos combustibles. Al mismo tiempo, fomenta el apoyo financiero para energías limpias. Este enfoque ayuda a los países a trabajar juntos con pasos claros basados en la ciencia para combatir el cambio climático.

**Próximo paso** – El nuevo panel científico seguirá asesorando a los gobiernos sobre las opciones energéticas. Los países involucrados considerarán las acciones recomendadas para planear cómo reducir el uso de combustibles fósiles. Esta cumbre podría motivar a más países a unirse a futuras reuniones y acelerar el cambio global hacia la energía limpia.

**En resumen** – Más de 50 países lanzaron en Colombia un esfuerzo guiado por la ciencia para dirigir la transición mundial de combustibles fósiles hacia fuentes de energía más limpias.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a glowing globe surrounded by diverse, interconnected scientific instruments and energy symbols—such as wind turbines, solar panels, and electric grids—emanating light beams that gently dissolve dark, fading oil rigs and coal mines in the background, all rendered in warm, natural earthy tones and soft blues, symbolizing global collaboration led by science to transition from fossil fuels to clean energy.

articles.title: Why cows burp methane: new ‘cellular organ’ discovered in gut microbes
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 30 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01425-8The discovery could help find ways to lower the amounts of methane produced by grazing animals.
articles.content:
The newly discovered organelle was found in ciliates (like this Entodinium caudatum). These eukaryotic organisms live in the rumen of herbivores.Credit: Chuanqi Jiang, Jinying He & Che Hu/Inst. of Hydrobiology, CAS

Scientists have identified a new type of cellular organelle inside microbes that live in the guts of ruminant animals such as sheep and cows.

In a paper published in Science on 30 April1, researchers describe an oval-shaped structure that they discovered inside rumen ciliates — a type of single-celled organism that lives inside ruminants.

These subcellular structures, called hydrogenobodies, were found to contribute to the generation of methane livestock. The organelle removes oxygen and releases hydrogen, which archaea in the rumen use as fuel to produce methane. Burping livestock contribute around 30% of global methane emissions produced by human activities, and the authors say their discovery could inspire new ways to reduce these emissions.

“This opens new opportunities to modulate the rumen microbiome more precisely” to make animals’ digestion more efficient and lower the methane they produce, says Oscar Gonzalez-Recio, a geneticist who studies the rumen microbiome at the University of Edinburgh, UK.

Tiny emitters

Ciliates make up 25% of microbial mass in the rumen — a specialized stomach compartment in ruminants that acts as a fermentation vat to break down plant matter. But these tiny organisms have been understudied because of technical challenges in isolating their DNA and analysing them without contamination from other organisms, says study co-author Wei Miao, a hydrobiologist at the Chinese Academy of Sciences in Wuhan, China.

Some of these organisms have tens of thousands of chromosomes, says Zhongtang Yu, a rumen microbiologist at Ohio State University in Columbus.

Previous studies had sequenced only 53 rumen ciliate genomes, says Yu, who was part of the first study to have sequenced the genome of a rumen ciliate in 20192.

Methane might be made by all living organisms

In the latest study, Miao and his colleagues collected ciliates from the rumens of cattle, sheep, goats and deer. By sequencing the genomes of those ciliates and combining their work with previously published genetic data sets, the team created a database comprising 450 ciliate genomes. The researchers also identified 65 species of rumen ciliates, 45 of which had not been genomically sequenced before.

“That's a very good, useful resource for us. We have now more genetic material that we can look into,” says Yu.

The presence of large amounts of two ciliate species was associated with high methane emissions when measured in 100 dairy cows. The researchers also found that sheep that produced high levels of methane had nearly 100 times the amount of a type of ciliates called Dasytricha compared to sheep fed the same diet that emitted low levels of methane.

Hydrogenobody organelles

Looking closely inside Dasytricha cells, the researchers identified a unique oval-shaped structure that they named the hydrogenobody. The inside of this organelle looked like a honeycomb matrix.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a new cellular organelle in gut microbes of ruminants that contributes to methane production, a major greenhouse gas. This breakthrough provides a promising pathway to develop methods to reduce methane emissions from livestock, which account for a substantial portion of global methane emissions. The discovery has broad environmental implications for climate change mitigation and is backed by detailed research and genomic data, making it a substantive and impactful advancement.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover new cell part in cow microbes linked to methane burps
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cows and other grazing animals have microbes in their stomachs called rumen ciliates that help them digest plants. These animals release methane, a gas that contributes to climate change, when they burp.

**What happened** – Researchers found a new part inside these microbes, called hydrogenobodies. These tiny oval structures help produce hydrogen, which other microbes use to make methane. The discovery came from studying the microbes in cows, sheep, goats, and deer, and analyzing hundreds of their genomes.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have identified this organelle, which plays a key role in methane production. Since livestock burps produce about 30% of human-caused methane emissions, understanding hydrogenobodies could help find ways to lower these emissions by changing the microbes in animals’ stomachs.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to use this new knowledge to develop methods that can reduce methane production in animals, possibly by targeting hydrogenobodies or the microbes that work with them. This could make farming more environmentally friendly.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The discovery of hydrogenobodies in gut microbes offers a new target to reduce methane emissions from cows and other grazing animals.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent une nouvelle partie cellulaire dans les microbes des vaches liée aux rots de méthane
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les vaches et autres animaux qui broutent ont des microbes dans leur estomac appelés ciliés du rumen qui les aident à digérer les plantes. Ces animaux rejettent du méthane, un gaz qui contribue au changement climatique, lorsqu’ils rotent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert une nouvelle partie à l’intérieur de ces microbes, appelée hydrogénobodies. Ces petites structures ovales aident à produire de l’hydrogène, que d’autres microbes utilisent pour fabriquer du méthane. Cette découverte provient de l’étude des microbes chez les vaches, moutons, chèvres et cerfs, et de l’analyse de centaines de leurs génomes.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques identifient cet organite, qui joue un rôle important dans la production de méthane. Comme les rots du bétail représentent environ 30 % des émissions de méthane causées par l’homme, comprendre les hydrogénobodies pourrait aider à réduire ces émissions en modifiant les microbes dans l’estomac des animaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent utiliser cette nouvelle connaissance pour développer des méthodes afin de réduire la production de méthane chez les animaux, en ciblant peut-être les hydrogénobodies ou les microbes qui travaillent avec eux. Cela pourrait rendre l’élevage plus respectueux de l’environnement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La découverte des hydrogénobodies dans les microbes intestinaux offre une nouvelle cible pour réduire les émissions de méthane des vaches et autres animaux qui broutent.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren una nueva parte celular en microbios de vacas relacionada con los eructos de metano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las vacas y otros animales que pastan tienen microbios en sus estómagos llamados ciliados del rumen que les ayudan a digerir plantas. Estos animales liberan metano, un gas que contribuye al cambio climático, cuando eructan.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron una nueva parte dentro de estos microbios, llamada hidrogenobodies. Estas pequeñas estructuras ovaladas ayudan a producir hidrógeno, que otros microbios usan para crear metano. El descubrimiento se hizo estudiando microbios en vacas, ovejas, cabras y ciervos, y analizando cientos de sus genomas.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos identifican este orgánulo, que juega un papel clave en la producción de metano. Como los eructos del ganado representan alrededor del 30 % de las emisiones humanas de metano, entender los hidrogenobodies podría ayudar a reducir estas emisiones cambiando los microbios en el estómago de los animales.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar este nuevo conocimiento para crear métodos que reduzcan la producción de metano en los animales, posiblemente atacando los hidrogenobodies o los microbios que trabajan con ellos. Esto podría hacer la ganadería más amigable con el ambiente.

**Resumen en una frase** – El descubrimiento de hidrogenobodies en microbios intestinales ofrece una nueva forma de reducir las emisiones de metano de vacas y otros animales que pastan.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting showing a large, gentle cow silhouette with a softly glowing, translucent rumen chamber visible inside, filled with vibrant, oval-shaped honeycomb-patterned organelles (hydrogenobodies) nestled within animated, single-celled ciliates floating peacefully, all rendered in natural earth tones and soft greens.

articles.title: Study finds Indonesian hotspots are 'like home' for endangered whale sharks
articles.summary: Two bays in Indonesia are hotspots for endangered whale sharks, who frequent their waters year-round and not seasonally as previously thought, a new study has revealed. The research tracked 70 whale sharks over a decade, shedding new light on their migration habits.
articles.content:
A whale shark swims off the coast of the Raja Ampat Islands in West Papua, Indonesia, on 19 March 2023.

A decade-long study tracking dozens of whale sharks off Indonesia's coast has revealed the secrets of their migrations and two hotspots where they gather year-round.

The research published Thursday fills in critical gaps in knowledge about the endangered species, and builds the case for increased protection of the Indonesian bays that the species flocks to, scientists said.

Whale sharks, which are a type of shark, not whale, face threats including pollution, tourism and vessel strikes.

While their speckled skin and stunning size make them instantly recognisable and a tourist draw, little was known about how the species moved between well-established gathering points.

Researchers in Indonesia aimed to change that by tagging Indo-Pacific whale sharks, which represent about 60 percent of the species, and tracing their movements.

Over a decade, they tagged 70 whale sharks at sites in Indonesia. Some were tracked for nearly three years.

"We could actually map or identify many variations of whale shark movement," said Mochamad Iqbal Herwata Putra, the study's lead author.

"The whale sharks that we tagged moved through (waters of) 13 different countries as well as the high seas," he told AFP.

The research, published in the Frontiers in Marine Science journal, also revealed that two bays in Indonesia host whale sharks year-round, rather than seasonally as previously thought.

"This is very unique," said Putra, focal species conservation senior manager at NGO Konservasi Indonesia.

While most "aggregation sites" for whale sharks are believed to be seasonal gathering points, Cenderawasih Bay off Indonesia's Papua province and Saleh Bay in Sumbawa are "like home for them", Putra said.

A combination of protection from predators and high availability of food such as krill make the bays crucial hotspots, with some evidence they may also function as nurseries.

Cenderawasih Bay is already protected as a national park, and its remote location has helped temper mass tourism.

But Saleh Bay is both popular with visitors and in a region with growing corn production on land and aquaculture at sea.

Both these industries can produce pollution – include pesticide runoff and sedimentation – that impacts water quality and whale sharks.

Putra noted that Indonesia recorded dozens of whale shark strandings in recent years. Pollution and interactions with fishing vessels are believed to be among the causes.

Konservasi Indonesia is working with the government to establish the country's first whale shark-specific marine protected area in Saleh Bay, which Putra said he hoped would come into effect this year.

(FRANCE 24 with AFP)
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about endangered whale sharks' year-round habitats in Indonesia, highlighting critical conservation hotspots. This new knowledge supports efforts to protect these areas, which benefits the species and marine biodiversity broadly. The study's decade-long scope and collaboration with conservation efforts demonstrate meaningful real-world impact and substance.
articles.personality_title: Study reveals two Indonesian bays are year-round homes for endangered whale sharks
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Whale sharks are the largest fish in the ocean and are endangered. Scientists have long known they gather in certain places, but they thought these visits were only seasonal.

**What happened** – Over ten years, researchers tagged and tracked 70 whale sharks in Indonesia. They discovered that two bays, Cenderawasih Bay and Saleh Bay, host whale sharks all year, not just part of the year. The sharks travel through waters of 13 countries but return regularly to these bays.

**Impact** – This finding is important because it shows these bays are like homes for whale sharks, offering food and safety. Knowing this helps protect the sharks better. Cenderawasih Bay is already a national park, but Saleh Bay faces threats from pollution and tourism. The study supports creating a special protected area in Saleh Bay to keep the sharks safe.

**What's next step** – Conservation groups and the Indonesian government aim to establish a whale shark marine protected area in Saleh Bay this year. This will help reduce pollution and protect the sharks’ habitat.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Two Indonesian bays serve as permanent homes for endangered whale sharks, guiding new efforts to protect these gentle giants year-round.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude révèle que deux baies indonésiennes sont des habitats permanents pour les requins-baleines menacés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les requins-baleines sont les plus grands poissons des océans et sont en danger. Les scientifiques savaient qu'ils se rassemblaient dans certains endroits, mais pensaient que c'était seulement à certaines saisons.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Pendant dix ans, des chercheurs ont marqué et suivi 70 requins-baleines en Indonésie. Ils ont découvert que deux baies, la baie de Cenderawasih et la baie de Saleh, accueillent ces requins toute l'année, pas seulement une partie de l'année. Les requins voyagent dans les eaux de 13 pays, mais reviennent régulièrement dans ces baies.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que ces baies sont comme des maisons pour les requins-baleines, offrant nourriture et sécurité. Cela aide à mieux protéger ces animaux. La baie de Cenderawasih est déjà un parc national, mais la baie de Saleh subit des menaces liées à la pollution et au tourisme. L'étude soutient la création d'une zone protégée spéciale dans la baie de Saleh.

**Prochaine étape** – Les groupes de conservation et le gouvernement indonésien veulent créer cette année une zone marine protégée dédiée aux requins-baleines dans la baie de Saleh. Cela aidera à réduire la pollution et à protéger leur habitat.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Deux baies indonésiennes sont des habitats permanents pour les requins-baleines menacés, guidant de nouvelles mesures pour les protéger toute l'année.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudio revela que dos bahías indonesias son hogares permanentes para tiburones ballena en peligro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los tiburones ballena son los peces más grandes del océano y están en peligro. Los científicos sabían que se reunían en ciertos lugares, pero pensaban que solo era en algunas temporadas.

**Qué pasó** – Durante diez años, investigadores marcaron y siguieron a 70 tiburones ballena en Indonesia. Descubrieron que dos bahías, la bahía de Cenderawasih y la bahía de Saleh, albergan a estos tiburones durante todo el año, no solo parte del año. Los tiburones viajan por aguas de 13 países, pero regresan regularmente a estas bahías.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra que estas bahías son como hogares para los tiburones ballena, ofreciendo alimento y seguridad. Conocer esto ayuda a protegerlos mejor. La bahía de Cenderawasih ya es un parque nacional, pero la bahía de Saleh enfrenta amenazas por contaminación y turismo. El estudio apoya la creación de un área protegida especial en la bahía de Saleh.

**Próximo paso** – Grupos de conservación y el gobierno de Indonesia planean establecer este año un área marina protegida para tiburones ballena en la bahía de Saleh. Esto ayudará a reducir la contaminación y proteger su hábitat.

**Resumen en una frase** – Dos bahías indonesias son hogares permanentes para tiburones ballena en peligro, guiando nuevos esfuerzos para protegerlos todo el año.
articles.image_prompt: A serene underwater scene showing a large, gently swimming whale shark with distinctive speckled skin near vibrant coral reefs inside a calm Indonesian bay, surrounded by schools of krill and softly glowing marine life, with distant silhouettes of islands under a warm, golden light filtering through clear turquoise waters.
